text: Under direct vision, the surgeon places two 5mm ports in the left lower quadrant and suprapubic midline. A disc of skin and subcutaneous fat is excised from the pre-marked stoma site in the right lower quadrant.

The patient is positioned in a moderately steep Trendelenburg with the left side elevated. The surgeon opens the left lateral peritoneal reflection alongside the distal sigmoid colon to identify and protect the left ureter.
The surgeon mobilizes the sigmoid and descending colon medially in a cephaloid direction by dissecting along the white line of Toldt, separating the colon from the lateral attachments.
The surgeon carefully navigates the medial aspect of the paracolic gutter to avoid inadvertent mobilization of the kidney during the colectomy procedure.

The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure by performing medial dissection of the omentum from the distal transverse colon and lateral dissection of the colon from the retroperitoneum. This reveals the body and tail of the pancreas and the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV).

The patient is positioned in the Trendelenburg with the right side elevated. The surgeon carefully moves the small bowel out of the pelvis to expose the right ureter, which is identified and protected.
The surgeon retracts the cecum in a cephalad and medial direction, scoring the peritoneum around the base of the cecum and terminal ileum to enter the correct retroperitoneal plane.

The surgeon develops the dissection plane medially to the inferior vena cava (IVC) and duodenum. The right lateral peritoneal reflection is opened, and the ascending colon is mobilized medially towards the duodenum.

The surgeon mobilizes the hepatic flexure until it is positioned below the level of the umbilicus, achieving full mobilization of the right colon to the midline. This exposes and protects the inferior vena cava and the duodenum.
The surgeon completes the medial mobilization of the hepatic flexure, detaching it from retroperitoneal structures and exposing the duodenum, with the gyrotis fascia visible medially.

The surgeon focuses on the pelvis, continuing dissection distally along the left lateral peritoneal reflection and left pararectal peritoneum. The precircular space is entered at the sacral promontory, with dissection proceeding distally within this space.

The surgeon scores the right parorectal peritoneum, ensuring the right ureter is identified and protected. The dissection is joined in the pre-sacral space with the previous dissection from the left side. Using careful retraction and exposure, a full circumferential dissection of the rectum is performed in the extramasorectal bloodless plane down to the pelvic floor.
A sponge stick is placed in the vagina to visually identify the rectovaginal septum, ensuring clear delineation of anatomical structures in a patient with a rectocele.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the rectum down to the top of the anal canal at the pelvic floor. The rectum is then transected using two firings of a laparoscopic linear articulated 45mm stapler.
The pneumoperitoneum is evacuated, and the entire colon is exteriorized from the terminal ileum to the transected edge of the rectum through the parumbilical incision.
The surgeon divides and ligates the mesentery using double ligatures on the larger vessels, while preserving the ileocolic pedicle and the omentum.
The surgeon transects the terminal ileum using a linear stapler approximately four centimeters from the ileocecal valve, and subsequently removes the specimen.

The surgeon exteriorizes the entire small bowel to examine it for signs of Crohn's disease. After inspection, the small bowel, except for the terminal ileum used for the pouch, is returned to the abdominal cavity, ensuring proper orientation.
A 15-centimeter J-pouch is constructed and returned to the abdominal cavity, with the surgeon ensuring the correct orientation of the cut edge of the pouch mesentery.

The surgeon gently dilates the anus and inserts the EEA stapler. The spike of the stapler is brought out adjacent to the staple line. The J-pouch is then maneuvered into the pelvis, and the anvil is docked into the stapler.
The surgeon re-approximates the EEA stapler before firing it and then removes it. A large round Jackson-Pratt drain is placed through the suprapubic port site.

The surgeon brings a loop of distal ileum above the constructed J-pouch to the premarked stoma site in the right lower quadrant. The ileostomy is matured using interrupted sutures, securing the bowel loop in place.
